references.cue: Linear’s March 12, 2026 UI refresh emphasizes calmer hierarchy, consistent headers, and dimmer navigation so the main work surface carries the attention.
references.adapted: The control-room routes keep restrained chrome, thin borders, and a dense reading plane so the approval or escalation stage stays dominant.
references.rejected: I did not copy Linear’s issue-detail layout or editor framing because the workspace still needs explicit consequence and trust markers.

references.cue: Vercel’s February 26, 2026 navigation rollout stresses a persistent sidebar, consistent tabs, and focus-preserving full-screen content.
references.adapted: Approval and escalation remain route-local shell extensions with stable section chrome and no detached sub-app.
references.rejected: I did not add another navigation tier or a dashboard-style analytics header because the shell already owns route-family navigation.

references.cue: Vercel’s dashboard docs reinforce persistent context, saved views, and detail panes that do not discard the surrounding shell.
references.adapted: The approval and escalation stages keep queue continuity and selected-anchor return instead of replacing the workspace.
references.rejected: I did not borrow project cards or chart-led summaries because the local blueprint requires consequence-first workflow, not dashboard reporting.

references.cue: Carbon’s table guidance emphasizes concise titles, explicit click targets, hover-assisted scanning, and disciplined toolbar actions.
references.adapted: Approval and escalation rows keep separate row-select and open-task actions, restrained hover states, and compact toolbars.
references.rejected: I did not turn the approval or escalation lanes into literal tables because the local shell needs richer narrative row anatomy than columnar data allows.

references.cue: NHS guidance stresses informative page titles, descriptive headings, strong focus styles, and not relying on colour or position alone.
references.adapted: The routes keep explicit headings, visible focus order, labelled controls, and text-backed urgency or freeze states.
references.rejected: I did not use icon-only urgency cues, hidden state changes, or colour-only warnings.

references.cue: NHS question-page guidance requires a sensible back path and preserving prior state rather than letting repeated actions replay invisibly.
references.adapted: Back/forward and reload preserve the same selected peer-route anchor and shell continuity instead of collapsing to a default row.
references.rejected: I did not turn approval review into a wizard because the operator still needs persistent source evidence and consequence context.

references.cue: DWP’s timeline notes keep the pattern intentionally simple and event-first, making it clear the timeline is a record of events rather than a navigation framework.
references.adapted: The urgent contact ladder is a restrained vertical record with dates, actor labels, and outcomes rather than a second workflow canvas.
references.rejected: I did not embed complex controls inside each timeline item because escalation actions belong in the promoted stage, not inside the chronology list.
